ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese beef and cheese curry rice. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents curry rice as a composite dish; used as closest parent since no specific beef-and-cheese curry rice code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Japanese beef and triple cheese curry rice. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this composite dish.

main_ingredients: triple cheese blend (typically processed cheese, mozzarella, cheddar or similar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with simmered beef in Japanese curry sauce and a generous triple cheese topping, as typically served at Sukiya-style Japanese curry rice restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef curry with rice (composite estimate) and USDA SR Legacy cheese and beef components; adapted for Japanese curry rice with triple cheese
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component-based calculation using USDA FoodData Central data for: (1) cooked Japanese short-grain rice (~55% of dish by weight), (2) simmered beef in curry sauce (~30% of dish by weight), and (3) triple cheese blend (~15% of dish by weight, using USDA data for processed/cheddar cheese). Energy and macros are cross-validated against published Japanese curry rice nutritional data from the Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) for curry rice base, with cheese and beef adjustments applied. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and curry-based composite dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for Japanese curry rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice. GI estimated at 62 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese-style curry rice (white rice base, GI ~60–65 range). The addition of beef protein and cheese fat is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72), consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation literature. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 89.1) / 100 ≈ 55.3, rounded to 55.4. Carbohydrate per serving = 19.8 g/100g × 450 g serving = 89.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry roux, cheese, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, representing 82–94% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, making this dish a significant sodium load that individuals with hypertension should consume only occasionally and in reduced portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large white rice portion combined with moderate GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 55 per serving is very high for a single meal, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting cauliflower rice or lower-GI grain alternatives, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and beef, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The triple cheese and beef topping contributes approximately 14.4 g saturated fat per serving, which approaches or exceeds the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g saturated fat, and the high sodium content further elevates cardiovascular risk, warranting portion control and infrequent consumption for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice is a Japanese-style curry rice dish featuring a generous portion of steamed short-grain Japanese rice topped with simmered beef in a rich, roux-based Japanese curry sauce and a triple cheese blend — typically a combination of processed cheese, mozzarella, and cheddar or similar melting cheeses. The dish is associated with the Sukiya chain, a well-known Japanese gyudon and curry restaurant brand with outlets in Singapore. Japanese curry (karē raisu) is characteristically milder, sweeter, and thicker than South Asian curries, with the roux providing a smooth, glossy sauce that coats the rice. The addition of triple cheese creates a creamy, umami-rich topping that melts into the curry sauce, adding richness and a dairy-forward flavour profile. The dish is calorie-dense and high in sodium due to the curry roux and cheese content. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 168 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.5 g fat (3.2 g saturated), and 19.8 g carbohydrate. A standard 450 g serving delivers roughly 756 kcal and approximately 1890 mg sodium. The glycemic load per serving is high (~55), driven by the large white rice base. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, high glycemic load, and saturated fat content. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten, dairy, or beef restrictions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or the Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) as a named composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by component-based calculation using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked Japanese short-grain rice, simmered beef, Japanese curry sauce, and cheese, cross-validated against MEXT 2020 curry rice base values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style curry rice. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as this is a composite rice-based curry dish with meat and cheese components, not a dairy-primary food. 2. The cuisine tag 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' in the input appears to be a data error from the source platform and has been discarded. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for this dish type. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level USDA data used for this estimation. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. Halal status is listed as false because Sukiya Singapore's halal certification status is not confirmed in publicly available regulatory records as of the processing date; consumers should verify directly with the restaurant. 7. The input cuisine tag 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' is inconsistent with the dish and has been excluded from the output. 8. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported in the source database for this estimated composite dish.
